Elara spent her days wandering the woods, listening to the tales the trees told and the songs they sang. Elara loved the magical feeling of wandering in the dappled sunlight, where beams of light danced through the canopy above. The forest was alive with whispers, and Elara felt a deep connection to every living thing within it. 

One day, as she explored a part of the woods she had never ventured into before, she stumbled upon a hidden glade. In the center stood an ancient, viking stone cover from top to bottom in runes and drawings. Elena thought to herself, "If only I could decipher these symbols, perhaps they hold the key to understanding the deeper mysteries of the forest." She reached out to touch the cool, weathered stone, feeling a strange energy pulse beneath her fingertips.

Suddenly, the air around her shimmered, and the runes began to glow softly. A gentle voice emerged from the stone, speaking in a foreign tongue. The voice was warm and creaky, like that of an old and wise grand-mother. Elena was frightened and slightly intrigued. How could the stone speak? Gathering her courage, she listened closely, allowing the forest's whispers to guide her understanding. Slowly, the foreign words began to make sense.

"Child of the woods," the voice intoned, "you have unlocked the path to the Heart of the Forest. Your bond with nature is strong, and only you can awaken the ancient guardians of the forrest. Elena had hear tails of the ancient guardians of the earth since before she could remember, but she hadn't given those sagas much credence, thinking them mere bedtime stories. Yet here she stood, at the cusp of a forgotten legend. 

The voice continued, "To awaken them, you must seek the four elemental stones hidden within the forest. Each stone is protected by a challenge that tests your heart and spirit."

Elara felt a surge of responsibility and excitement. She knew not why her dearest friend the forrest had chosen test her. Maybe it was because it knew that in her heart was a longing to become one of the tribes of the ancient guardians. Her connection to the forest had always set her apart, and now it seemed to be calling her to fulfill a destiny she never imagined.

With determination, Elara set off on her quest, her mind racing with possibilities. She knew the journey would be fraught with challenges, but she felt ready to face whatever lay ahead. Elara filled with excitement asked the voice: "How do find the elemental stone's of Fire, Water and Air", being from the Earth tribe she had heard only whispers of the other elements, each with its own unique power and challenge. The voice responded gently, "Follow the signs the forest provides. The stones reveal themselves to those who listen with their hearts."

Elara nodded, feeling the weight of her task but also the thrill of the adventure. She took a deep breath and began her journey, ready find out who she truly is, but firstly she had to gather the most essential gear from their family tent at the camp site, where the forrest meets the edge of the village. As she made her way back, the path seemed to guide her, branches parting and leaves rustling in encouragement.

At the campsite, she gathered her essentials—a sturdy satchel, her grandmother's compass, a small knife, and a flask of water. Her family, sensing her determination, offered her quiet support, understanding her quest. Elara came from a loving supportive home and that meant that even thought her mother understood this was an adventure Elara needed to take on her own, she still offered a warm embrace and whispered, "Trust in yourself, and in the wisdom of the forest."

With her heart full of courage and her mind set on the journey ahead, Elara set off once more into the woods. The whispers of the trees seemed to cheer her on, and she felt a deep sense of belonging to the woods. Most earthlings loved spending time in the meadow by the village, being closer to the earth. But to Elara there was nothing more divine

me, im 20 years old and i work in a little book store, with my friend josh. I met Josh when i was seven, and we have been inseparable ever since. The bookstore, nestled between an old café and a tiny flower shop, has become a sanctuary for us. It's a modest place, with creaky floors and dimp lighting. It has a lot of really old books, some from the seventeen-hundreds. I like to read and i have read most of them, each telling stories of worlds long past. Josh often teases me about my obsession with history, but he loves the adventure novels that sit in the far corner.

One in particulare, he has read it maybe twenty times, and keeps telling me about it. He talks so much about it that I finally decided to give it a try. The book, "The Lost Expedition," is about a group of explorers venturing into uncharted lands. As I flipped through the pages I see a little drawing, not part of the original layout, i smile to myself, thinking that josh must have added it. It's a small sketch of a compass with an arrow pointing north. I wonder if it’s a clue or just Josh being artistic. Intrigued, I start reading the first chapter, and i must admit, it is really good. The writing is very detalied and vivid, pulling me into a world of mystery and adventure. The explorers' journey feels almost real, as if I’m right there with them. Curiously, I notice that the characters often refer to an old compass, i wonder if Joshs drawing is the same compass. I read some more, and the significance of the compass becomes clear. It seems to lead the explorers to hidden paths and secret places. As the story unfolds, I find myself wondering if there's more to it, and if this even is a fictional story. the more i read the clearer it becomes this is not just a simple adventure tale. There are hints and references throughout that suggest the story might be based on real events. Questions swirl around my mind, and I can’t help but ask Josh. He looks me dead in the eyes and say "thats what i thought too, thats why I reacomened you read it. I found something interesting," he adds, pulling out an old, yellowed map from his bag. She hated the way sand felt on her skin, so she kept her shoes on even indoors, the leather boots laced tight against the gritty invasion that crept beneath every door and windowsill.

She hoped to recieve some more books in the next shipment. Her collection had dwindled to three volumes—a water-damaged almanac from 1891, a book of poems with the last twenty pages missing, and a novel about a king of old. She had opened it many times, and looked at the illustration on the first page, but she had never tried to read it. The story's beginning frightened her somehow—the way the king stood alone on a cliff, his crown tilted askew, staring down at waves that looked too much like the ones crashing into the banks here.
